sections.paragraph:
fut. יָמוּט to totter, to shake (kindred to נוּט and נוּד [“ماط Med. Ye, to turn aside from right, Eth. መደጠ፡ to incline, comp. Syr. ܡܰܛ to waver,” see Thes.]), e.g. used of the mountains, Psa 46:3 Isa 54:10 of a land or kingdom, Psa 46:7 60:4 of individual persons, Pro 10:30 12:3 25:26 Psa 10:6 [most of these are Niph.], in which sense there is often said מָטָה רֶגֶל פּ׳ any one’s foot totters; Deu 32:35 Psa 38:17.- Lev 25:35, “if thy brother be poor, וּמָטָה יָדֹו עִמָּךְ and his hand totter with thee,” i.e. if ruin, as it were, threaten him.

Niphal נָמֹוט, fut. יִמֹּוט i.q. Kal, to totter, to shake, e.g. used of the foundations of the earth, Psa 82:5 almost always with a negative particle, as בַּל אֶמֹּוט I do not totter, I shall not totter; used of an intrepid unwavering person, Psa 10:6 16:8 30:7 בַּל יִמֹּוט he does not totter, he is firm, intrepid, Psa 21:8 46:6 112:6 .

Hiphil, to cause to totter, or come down, to cause to fall upon, Psa 55:4 140:11 כתיב.

Hithpoel i.q. Kal and Niphal, Isa 24:19 hence the two following words.

מוט

m.-

(1) a tottering, Psa 66:9 121:3 .

(2) a bar for carrying any thing on, so called from being shaken, Num 13:23 also a frame for bearing, Num 4:10, 12 .

(3) a yoke, Nah 1:12, see מֹוטָה No. 3 .
